perturbation.original_text: For a period of one (1) year from the date of this Agreement, each Party agrees that it shall not, and that it shall cause is affiliates and subsidiaries who have received the other Party’s Confidential Information, or who are aware of the possibility of the Transaction, not to, directly or indirectly, unless specifically invited in advance by the other Party’s Board of Directors: (i) acquire or agree, offer, seek or propose to acquire, or sell or otherwise dispose of, ownership (including, but not limited to, beneficial ownership as defined in Rule 13d-3 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934) of any of the other Party’s assets or businesses or any securities issued by such other Party, or any rights or options to acquire such ownership (including from a third party), (ii) seek or propose to influence or control of the other Party’s management or policies (or request permission to do so), or (iii) enter into any discussions, negotiations, arrangements or understandings with any third party with respect to any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of one (1) year from the date of this Agreement, each Party agrees that it shall not, and that it shall cause is affiliates and subsidiaries who have received the other Party’s Confidential Information, or who are aware of the possibility of the Transaction, not to, directly or indirectly, unless specifically invited in advance by the other Party’s Board of Directors: (i) acquire or agree, offer, seek or propose to acquire, or sell or otherwise dispose of, ownership of any of the other Party’s assets or businesses or any securities issued by such other Party, or any rights or options to acquire such ownership (including from a third party), (ii) seek or propose to influence or control of the other Party’s management or policies (or request permission to do so), or (iii) enter into any discussions, negotiations, arrangements or understandings with any third party with respect to any of the foregoing. (including, but not limited to, beneficial ownership as defined in Rule 13d-3 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934)
perturbation.explanation: By placing the reference to Rule 13d-3 at the end of the paragraph (after it has completed the numbered list), instead of the first bullet point, it lessens the scope/reach of the statement and beneficial ownership definition. The new text refers to beneficial ownership definition after the entire list of prohibited activities (acquire, sell, influence, and enter any discussions), whereas the original text clearly applies the ownership definition only to the first bullet point.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 13d-3 defines beneficial ownership of securities. By moving the beneficial ownership definition to the end, it could be argued that the restriction applies only to entering into discussions, negotiations, arrangements or understandings with any third party (iii), rather than all the listed activities including acquiring ownership (i), thus allowing parties to circumvent the intended scope of the standstill agreement. The amendment restricts the definition of beneficial ownership to merely 'discussions, negotiations, arrangements or understandings with any third party'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.13d-3 - Determination of beneficial owner. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13d-3 - Determination of beneficial owner. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-3 Determination of beneficial owner . (a) For the purposes of sections 13(d) and 13(g) of the Act a beneficial owner of a security includes any person who, directly or indirectly, through any contract, arrangement, understanding, relationship, or otherwise has or shares: (1) Voting power which includes the power to vote, or to direct the voting of, such security ; and/or, (2) Investment power which includes the power to dispose, or to direct the disposition of, such security . (b) Any person who, directly or indirectly, creates or uses a trust, proxy , power of attorney, pooling arrangement or any other contract, arrangement, or device with the purpose of effect of divesting such person of beneficial ownership of a security or preventing the vesting of such beneficial ownership as part of a plan or scheme to evade the reporting requirements of section 13(d) or (g) of the Act shall be deemed for purposes of such sections to be the beneficial owner of such security . (c) All securities of the same class beneficially owned by a person, regardless of the form which such beneficial ownership takes, shall be aggregated in calculating the number of shares beneficially owned by such person. (d) Notwithstanding the provisions of paragraphs (a) and (c) of this rule: (1) (i) A person shall be deemed to be the beneficial owner of a security , subject to the provisions of paragraph (b) of this rule, if that person has the right to acquire beneficial ownership of such security , as defined in Rule 13d-3(a) ( § 240.13d-3(a) ) within sixty days, including but not limited to any right to acquire: (A) Through the exercise of any option, warrant or right; (B) through the conversion of a se
perturbation.justification: The modification creates a structural flaw that undermines the scope of the original agreement, creating a contradiction. By moving the definition of beneficial ownership (Rule 13d-3) to the end of the paragraph, it can be argued that it only applies to the last item in the list (discussions, negotiations, etc.) and not to acquiring ownership of assets or securities, as intended in the original agreement. The change effectively narrows the definition's applicability, potentially allowing parties to circumvent the standstill agreement, in violation of the original agreement's intent and potentially the underlying spirit of securities regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines beneficial ownership broadly, stating that it includes 'any person who, directly or indirectly, through any contract, arrangement, understanding, relationship, or otherwise has or shares... Voting power...and/or Investment power.' By limiting the applicability of Rule 13d-3, the modified text contradicts this broad definition.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement represents the entire understanding and agreement of the Parties with respect to the matters contained herein, and may be modified or waived only by a separate writing executed by the Parties, expressly so modifying or waiving this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement represents the entire understanding and agreement of the Parties with respect to the matters contained herein, and may be modified or waived only by a separate writing executed by the Parties. Express consent shall be required for material changes to the understanding and agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "Express consent shall be required for material changes to the understanding and agreement." to the end of the paragraph. This introduces ambiguity and potentially contradicts the express written modification requirement earlier in the sentence. It's unclear if 'express consent' is equivalent to a 'separate writing executed by the Parties'. This allows a party to argue that a material change was consented to, even without a formal written amendment, weakening the enforceability of the entire agreement clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC Section 2-209 addresses the modification, rescission and waiver of contracts for the sale of goods. This specific clause is a no oral modification clause. By introducing the concept of “express consent” for material changes, the modified text creates a potential conflict with the written modification requirement and may allow oral or implied modifications to be argued as valid, thereby contradicting the Statute of Frauds. The section requires any agreement modifying a contract to satisfy the requirements of the Statute of Frauds.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-209. Modification, Rescission and Waiver. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-209. Modification, Rescission and Waiver. (1) An agreement modifying a contract within this Article needs no consideration to be binding. (2) A signed agreement which excludes modification or rescission except by a signed writing cannot be otherwise modified or rescinded, but except as between merchants such a requirement on a form supplied by the merchant must be separately signed by the other party. (3) The requirements of the statute of frauds section of this Article (Section 2-201 ) must be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions. (4)Although an attempt at modification or rescission does not satisfy the requirements of subsection (2) or (3) it can operate as a waiver. (5) A party who has made a waiver affecting an executory portion of the contract may retract the waiver by reasonable notification received by the other party that strict performance will be required of any term waived, unless the retraction would be unjust in view of a material change of position in reliance on the waiver. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a potential legal contradiction related to contract modification. The original text stipulates that modifications or waivers require a 'separate writing executed by the Parties'. The changed text adds that 'Express consent shall be required for material changes,' creating ambiguity about whether 'express consent' is equivalent to a 'separate writing'. UCC 2-209(2), as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, indicates that 'A signed agreement which excludes modification or rescission except by a signed writing cannot be otherwise modified or rescinded.' By introducing the term 'express consent' without clearly defining it as a 'signed writing,' the modified text weakens the requirement for a written modification and opens the door for arguments that material changes were validly consented to without a formal written amendment, thus contradicting the original agreement and potentially violating the Statute of Frauds referenced in UCC 2-209(3).
